evidence_text: Only 466 of the scanned repositories, or 5%, had adopted at least one of the AI configuration file formats the study considered.
source_excerpt: "Only 466 (5%) of the repositories that we scanned had already adopted at least one of the formats that we considered."
note: The reported adoption rate is for the file formats included in this study.
caveat: The reported adoption rate is for the file formats included in this study.

evidence_text: The adoption analysis covered four commercial tools and did not include other tools such as Cline or OpenCode.
source_excerpt: "However, we focused only on four commercial tools, although other commercial or open-source alternatives such as Cline and OpenCode exist."
note: This bounds the adoption result because the measured 5% rate excludes other AI configuration ecosystems the paper names as possible future additions.
caveat: This bounds the adoption result because the measured 5% rate excludes other AI configuration ecosystems the paper names as possible future additions.

evidence_text: Among repositories with AI configuration files, the language counts reported were 135 TypeScript, 58 Go, 58 Python, 56 C#, 36 Java, 34 JavaScript, 32 C++, 29 Rust, 19 PHP, and 9 C.
source_excerpt: "We found AI configuration files in 135 repositories with TypeScript as main language, 58 Go, 58 Python, 56 C#, 36 Java, 34 JavaScript, 32 C++, 29 Rust, 19 PHP, and 9 C."
note: These are counts for repositories with AI configuration files, not overall language prevalence on GitHub.
caveat: These are counts for repositories with AI configuration files, not overall language prevalence on GitHub.

evidence_text: Copilot instruction files were the longest on average at 310 lines, while AGENTS.md files showed the highest length variation with mean 142 lines and standard deviation 231.
source_excerpt: "The Copilot instruction files were on average the longest ( M = 310 lines, SD = 127 lines)... AGENTS.md files had the highest variation ( M = 142, SD = 231 )."
note: The source reports file length in lines and explicitly distinguishes average length from variation.
caveat: The source reports file length in lines and explicitly distinguishes average length from variation.

evidence_text: For the AGENTS.md heading analysis, the authors excluded 15 files created before January 1, 2025 and excluded 5 files with no heading structure.
source_excerpt:
"We excluded 15 AGENTS.md files that were created before January 1, 2025, i.e., before the AGENTS.md convention was introduced."
"Five repositories contained AGENTS.md files without any heading structure; these were excluded from the analysis."
note: These exclusions limit the structural analysis to post-convention files that also use headings.
caveat: These exclusions limit the structural analysis to post-convention files that also use headings.

evidence_text: The three most common level-1 or level-2 heading categories in AGENTS.md were conventions/best practices (50), contribution guidelines (48), and architecture/project structure (47).
note: The counts are heading-category frequencies at levels 1 or 2, as reported in Table 1.
caveat: The counts are heading-category frequencies at levels 1 or 2, as reported in Table 1.

evidence_text: The authors characterized AGENTS.md writing style along five dimensions: descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional.
source_excerpt: "We found that the writing style can be characterized along five stylistic dimensions: descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional."
note: The source presents these as observed stylistic dimensions in the analyzed sections.
caveat: The source presents these as observed stylistic dimensions in the analyzed sections.

evidence_text: The source gives "Never commit directly to the main branch" as an example of a prohibitive instruction in AGENTS.md.
source_excerpt: "Prohibitive statements were also common, explicitly indicating what not to do, e.g., 'Never commit directly to the main branch.'"
note: This is presented as an example of prohibitive style, not as a frequency estimate.
caveat: This is presented as an example of prohibitive style, not as a frequency estimate.

evidence_text: Among 155 AGENTS.md files, 77 had no changes after the initial commit, 36 changed once, and 32 changed between two and seven times.
source_excerpt: "77 (50%) of them had not been changed after the initial commit, 36 (23%) only once, and 32 (21%) between two and seven times."
note: These counts describe observed post-creation change frequency in the sampled AGENTS.md histories.
caveat: These counts describe observed post-creation change frequency in the sampled AGENTS.md histories.

evidence_text: The detailed evolution analysis focused on 10 files with at least 10 commits, producing a sample of 169 commits, or 37% of the 453 collected commits.
source_excerpt: "For this study, we decided to focus on 10 files (6%) with at least 10 commits, which yielded a sample of 169 commits to annotate (37% of the 453 collected commits)."
note: This narrows the commit-category analysis to the most frequently changed files rather than all AGENTS.md histories.
caveat: This narrows the commit-category analysis to the most frequently changed files rather than all AGENTS.md histories.

evidence_text: Most annotated commits, 111 out of 169 or 66%, represented only one change category.
source_excerpt: "most commits (111, 66%) represented only one change category."
note: The unit of analysis here is commit-level category assignment in the annotated sample.
caveat: The unit of analysis here is commit-level category assignment in the annotated sample.

evidence_text: The most frequent AGENTS.md change categories were add instruction(s) with 78 instances and modify instruction(s) with 59 instances.
source_excerpt: "Only 466 (5%) of the repositories that we scanned had already adopted at least one of the formats that we considered."
note: The reported adoption rate is for the file formats included in this study.
caveat: The reported adoption rate is for the file formats included in this study.
